data.content:
Cholesterol Level
The serum cholesterol level, measured in mg, used in association studies concerning cardiac risk.

Blood Pressure
Blood pressure is a physiological measure that, when transformed by the logarithm (blood pressure – 75), can define a variable for statistical analysis.<SEP>Blood pressure is a variable measured in mm Hg that is correlated with risk.<SEP>The systolic blood pressure, measured in mm Hg, used in association studies concerning cardiac risk.<SEP>Blood Pressure is a critical measurement point for managing chronic ischaemic heart disease.<SEP>A physiological measurement that can be improved by exercise training and is a risk factor for CAD.

data.entity_name: Risk of Developing Coronary Heart Disease
data.content:
Risk of Developing Coronary Heart Disease
The outcome variable whose increase is analyzed in relation to cholesterol and blood pressure levels.

Framingham Population
The specific population used in the study to describe the relationships between risk, cholesterol, and blood pressure.

Discriminant Functions
Discriminant functions is a mathematical method that substitutes a formula to describe theoretical frequency distributions based on observed frequencies.<SEP>Mathematical functions used to describe the relationships among risk and the two variables (cholesterol and blood pressure).

5th Percentile
The lower quartile threshold for either cholesterol or blood pressure, used for risk comparisons.

95th Percentile
The upper quartile threshold for either cholesterol or blood pressure, used for risk comparisons.

1% Difference
A standardized unit of change used to quantify the difference in either cholesterol or blood pressure and its corresponding effect on risk.

2.66% Difference
The percentage difference in risk associated with a 1% change in cholesterol, measured relative to a given blood pressure.

4.62% Difference
The percentage difference in risk associated with a 1% change in systolic blood pressure, measured relative to a given cholesterol level.

175 mm Hg
A specific systolic blood pressure value at which a 1% difference is associated with a 2.57% difference in risk.

110 mm Hg
The starting systolic blood pressure value used as a reference point for assessing the 4.62% difference in risk.

CHD Population
The CHD Population is the group whose risk of developing new cases is being studied, used for deriving risk coefficients.<SEP>The CHD population is one of the two populations studied, whose distributions (for cholesterol and blood pressure) are used to deduce the risk function.

NCHD Population
The NCHD Population is a comparison group used in the calculation of risk coefficients, alongside the CHD Population.<SEP>The NCHD population is the second population studied, used in conjunction with the CHD population for statistical analysis.

Serum Cholesterol
A physiological measurement variable used in calculating CHD risk, measured in mg/100 cc.<SEP>Serum cholesterol is a measurable value used to calculate the risk function, P(Y).<SEP>Serum cholesterol is a variable measured in the population to determine the associated risk of coronary heart disease.

Systolic Blood Pressure
A physiological measurement variable used in calculating CHD risk, measured in mm Hg.<SEP>Systolic blood pressure is a variable measured in the population used to analyze the risk of coronary heart disease.

Equation 5
A mathematical formula (P = 1/[1 + exp{ -( + X + X ) } ]) used to model a probability (P).

Equation 6
A linear mathematical version of Equation 5 (log P/(1 - P) = + X + X) used to study quantitative risk dependence.

Discriminant Function
The right-hand side of Equation 6, whose numerical value can discriminate between individuals likely and unlikely to become members of the CHD population.

Table 2
A summary table detailing the New CHD in 6

Variances
The Variances are statistical measures of variation used in the computation of coefficients related to risk modeling.

Correlation Coefficient
The Correlation Coefficient is a measure of the linear relationship between two variables, used in risk calculation models.

Confidence Limits
Formulas providing approximate limits for the coefficients used in the CHD risk model.

Log10 Cholesterol
A derived variable used in the model, representing the base-10 logarithm of cholesterol levels.<SEP>Log10 cholesterol is the base-10 logarithm of cholesterol, used as a key variable in the log normal and bivariate frequency distribution models.

Log (Blood Pressure - 75)
A derived variable used in the model, representing the base-10 logarithm of blood pressure minus 75.

Men 40-59
The demographic group that is the subject population for the tracking of New CHD cases presented in Table 2.

Cholesterol
Cholesterol is a variable measured in mg/100 cc that is correlated with risk.

Risk
Risk is the measure of potential health outcomes that depends on both cholesterol and blood pressure levels.

Bivariate Normal Model
The model is a statistical tool used to summarize the joint dependence of risk on cholesterol and blood pressure.

Equation 7
Equation 7 is a mathematical formula used to describe the association between risk and changes in cholesterol and blood pressure.

Expected Number Of CHD Cases
This data represents the anticipated number of new Coronary Heart Disease cases, compared against actual observations.

Percentage Change
Percentage change is a calculation method used to determine the difference in risk relative to the initial level, often independent of the starting point.

Absolute Change
Absolute change is a calculation method describing how the change in risk depends on the level from which the change takes place.

X1
X1 is the data input representing serum cholesterol levels in mg/100 cc, used in the risk calculation formula.

X2
X2 is the data input representing systolic blood pressure levels in mm Hg, used in the risk calculation formula.

L-Year Risk
The l-year risk is a measurement derived as one-sixth of the 6-year risk.

mg/100 cc
The unit of measurement for serum cholesterol levels.

mm Hg
The unit of measurement for systolic blood pressure levels.

Log Normal Distribution
The log normal distribution is an excellent theoretical distribution proposed to characterize the relationship between cholesterol levels in both CHD and NCHD populations.

Risk Function P(Y)
The risk function P(Y) denotes the proportion of individuals with serum cholesterol of Y that belong to the CHD population.

Bivariate Normal Frequency Function
This function is used to characterize both the CHD and NCHD populations simultaneously using log10 cholesterol and log10 (blood pressure – 75).

Framingham
The Framingham analysis provided initial results regarding CHD and NCHD distributions, particularly concerning cholesterol and blood pressure.

Proportion P
Proportion P represents the probability of an individual belonging to the CHD population, calculated as 92/1329.

Log10 Blood Pressure Adjusted
Log10 (blood pressure – 75) is the base-10 logarithm of blood pressure minus 75, serving as a key variable in the bivariate frequency distribution models.

Jerome Cornfield
Jerome Cornfield is the author of the paper describing the joint dependence of coronary heart disease risk on serum cholesterol and systolic blood pressure.

Coronary Heart Disease
Coronary Heart Disease is the disease whose risk is being analyzed based on serum cholesterol and systolic blood pressure levels.<SEP>Coronary heart disease is described as a serious public health problem with a multifactorial etiology.<SEP>Coronary heart disease is a health risk increased by central obesity, type 1 and type 2 diabetes, and smoking.

Discriminant Function Analysis
Discriminant Function Analysis is a mathematical method used to summarize observations and model the joint dependence of CHD risk on cholesterol and blood pressure.

Framingham, Massachusetts
Framingham, Massachusetts is the location where the long-term follow-up study of heart disease was conducted.

National Heart Institute
The National Heart Institute is the organization where Jerome Cornfield was affiliated for the study.

Bethesda, Maryland
Bethesda, Maryland is the location where the National Heart Institute is situated.

data.entity_name: Federation of American Societies for Experimental Biology
data.content:
Federation of American Societies for Experimental Biology
The Federation of American Societies for Experimental Biology is the organization associated with the publication details of the study.

Washington, D.C.
Washington, D.C. is the location of the Federation of American Societies for Experimental Biology.

Federation Proceedings
Federation Proceedings is the source publication where the paper was presented in July-August 1962.

Myocardial Infarction
Myocardial infarction is a clinically manifest form of coronary heart disease developed in the study sample.<SEP>Myocardial infarction is a significant medical event requiring long-term treatment with aspirin and beta-adrenergic blockers.

Angina Pectoris
Angina pectoris is a clinically manifest form of coronary heart disease developed in the study sample.<SEP>A chest pain condition that can be effectively treated by calcium antagonists, primarily by relieving vasospastic angina.<SEP>Angina pectoris is mentioned as the most common initial manifestation of chronic ischaemic heart disease.<SEP>Angina Pectoris is a symptom whose threshold is assessed and which can be managed by therapy to improve quality of life.

6-Year Risk
The l-year risk is defined by its mathematical relationship to the 6-year risk, specifically being one-sixth of it.

Dihydropyridine
One of the common classes of calcium antagonists used for treating conditions like hypertension and angina pectoris.

Calcium Antagonists
A class of drugs used to treat hypertension and angina pectoris, which decreases coronary vascular resistance and increases coronary blood flow.<SEP>Calcium antagonists are a class of drugs that effectively treat hypertension and angina pectoris by decreasing coronary vascular resistance.<SEP>Calcium antagonists, including verapamil and diltiazem, are medications used for cardiac treatment, especially if beta-blockers are contraindicated.<SEP>Calcium antagonists may be selectively prescribed in patients with specific indications such as hypertension or angina.

Nondihydropyridine
One of the common classes of calcium antagonists used for treating conditions like hypertension and angina pectoris.

Hypertension
A chronic condition characterized by high blood pressure and listed as a modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease.<SEP>Hypertension is a major cardiovascular risk factor, a contraindication, and a condition treated by certain medications.<SEP>Hypertension is defined by a blood pressure exceeding 170/100 mmHg, indicating a high-risk condition for severe ACS.<SEP>Hypertension is a health condition with recorded prevalence rates in adults aged 25 and above.<SEP>Hypertension is a risk factor associated with central obesity, potentially leading to increased coronary artery disease.<SEP>Hypertension (HBP) is a risk factor requiring blood pressure control goals (≤ 140/90 mmHg).<SEP>Hypertension is a specific indication that might warrant the selective prescription of calcium antagonists.

Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)
A serious disease state whose risk factors, such as smoking and hyperlipidemia, are modifiable and affect clinical outcome.

Obesity
A common condition associated with an increased risk for CAD and mortality, contributing to other coronary risk factors.<SEP>Obesity is a condition that can be assessed by measures such as Body Mass Index, Waist Hip Ratio, and Waist Circumference.

Beta-blockers
A class of drugs that may be used in combination with or substituted for calcium antagonists for initial angina therapy.

Verapamil
A specific calcium antagonist that reduces myocardial oxygen demand by decreasing heart rate and contractility.

Diltiazem
A specific calcium antagonist that reduces myocardial oxygen demand by decreasing heart rate and contractility.

Fibrate Gemfibrozil
A drug that was demonstrated in a randomized trial to reduce cardiovascular mortality in patients with CAD.

World Health Organisation
The international body responsible for health standards, featured on the document source citation.<SEP>The World Health Organisation is an organization listed in the context header related to medical care standards.<SEP>The World Health Organisation is listed as a source for health information regarding coronary artery disease prevention.<SEP>The World Health Organisation is an organization that recommends the Body Mass Index for assessing the degree of obesity.

Mini-Invasive Heart
The core area of study related to treating and managing conditions like CAD, focused on prevention and risk reduction.

Medicare
Placeholder for a clinical outcome measure or system related to patient care standards.

Diabetes Mellitus
A modifiable coronary disease risk factor that, alongside smoking and hypertension, is common in patients with chronic ischaemic heart disease.<SEP>Diabetes Mellitus is a health condition with recorded prevalence rates in adults aged 25 and above.

Hyperlipidemia
A modifiable coronary disease risk factor that is associated with elevated triglycerides and low HDL cholesterol.<SEP>Hyperlipidemia is identified as a cardiac risk factor that requires control to aid in secondary prevention of ACS.

Cigarette Smoking
A modifiable coronary disease risk factor that increases the risk of death from CAD.

HDL Cholesterol
A measure of cholesterol level; low levels are associated with increased CAD risk.<SEP>High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol is measured for lipid management, with a recommended value of > 35 mg/dl.

Triglycerides
A type of fat in the blood; elevated levels are associated with increased CAD risk.

Systemic Vascular Resistance
A physiological measure whose reduction is a primary mechanism by which certain calcium antagonists decrease myocardial oxygen demand.

Diuretics
A class of medication, such as Furosemide, used in the therapy for Congestive Heart Failure.<SEP>Diuretics are recommended to initiate therapy for congestive heart failure.

Nitrates
Nitrates are part of the recommended therapy for congestive heart failure and recurrent angina.

Positive Inotropic Agents
Positive inotropic agents, such as Dobutamine and Dopamine, are used in the therapy for congestive heart failure.

Dobutamine
A positive inotropic agent used in therapy for severe heart failure.<SEP>Dobutamine is a positive inotropic agent used intravenously in the treatment of congestive heart failure.

Dopamine
A positive inotropic agent used in therapy for severe heart failure.<SEP>Dopamine is a positive inotropic agent used intravenously in the treatment of congestive heart failure.

Swan-Ganz Pulmonary Artery Catheter
A specialized catheter used to monitor pulmonary artery pressures in severe cardiac cases.<SEP>The Swan-Ganz catheter is a device placed in patients with severe ventricular dysfunction or cardiogenic shock to measure multiple physiological resistances and output.

Aspirin
Aspirin is a common drug given to all patients with suspected ACS unless contraindicated.<SEP>Aspirin is a foundational agent and a mainstay used in the treatment of ACS.<SEP>A drug administered immediately upon the patient's arrival in the emergency ward for acute MI.<SEP>A drug administered as early adjunctive therapy to prevent thromboxane A2 and prostacyclin production in platelets and endothelial cells.<SEP>Aspirin is recommended for long-term treatment of myocardial infarction and recurrent angina.

Beta-Adrenergic Blockers
A class of drugs used to reduce myocardial oxygen consumption and lower morbidity rates.<SEP>Beta-adrenergic blockers are recommended for long-term treatment of myocardial infarction and recurrent angina.

Intravenous Nitroglycerin
Intravenous nitroglycerin is recommended for treating recurrent angina.

Heparin
Heparin is an anticoagulant therapy used in ACS management, requiring monitoring of KPTT.<SEP>A blood-thinning medication recommended for patients with specific forms of MI or thrombus.<SEP>Heparin is recommended as

Furosemide
Furosemide is a diuretic used in the treatment of congestive heart failure.

Oxygen
A substance administered by nasal prongs or masks to acute MI patients.<SEP>Oxygen is a therapy component recommended for congestive heart failure.

Analgesics
Analgesics are recommended for treating recurrent angina.

ACE Inhibitors
A drug class that should be initiated early in patients with acute MI to improve left ventricular function.<SEP>ACE Inhibitors are recommended as part of secondary prevention in patients with left ventricular dysfunction.

Carvedilol
Carvedilol has been shown to attenuate ventricular remodeling in patients with left ventricular dysfunction after MI.

Congestive Heart Failure
Congestive heart failure is a condition requiring initial therapy with diuretics, nitrates, and oxygen.

Left Ventricular Dysfunction
Left ventricular dysfunction is a medical condition requiring secondary prevention using ACE inhibitors and monitoring for drug effects.

Angina
Angina is a symptom of myocardial oxygen demand exceeding supply, and it can manifest as stable angina or unstable angina.<SEP>Angina refers to the status of pain that can present as new-onset, increasing severity, or post-infarction.<SEP>Angina is a symptom that requires treatment with intravenous nitroglycerin and is monitored with calcium antagonists.

Cardiovascular Disease
CVD is the central condition addressed in the guidelines, detailing risk factor reduction and interventions.<SEP>Cardiovascular disease is a major global public health concern with rising mortality rates worldwide.

Coronary Artery Disease
A general concept referring to the condition where the coronary arteries are affected, ranging from stable to acute syndromes.<SEP>Coronary artery disease is identified as the primary non-communicable health problem in Mauritius.

Mauritius
Mauritius is the location where Coronary Artery Disease is identified as a primary non-communicable health problem.

Primary Care Physician
The primary care physician is responsible for preliminary diagnosis, risk stratification, and initial management of patients.<SEP>A medical doctor who may co-manage patients with a cardiologist.<SEP>The primary care physician is responsible for regularly checking for risk factors such as smoking and elevated lipid levels.

data.entity_name: Ministry Of Health And Quality Of Life/Mauritius Institute Of Health
data.content:
Ministry Of Health And Quality Of Life/Mauritius Institute Of Health
A governmental/health body listed in the context header regarding medical services and standards.<SEP>This organization is listed as a source for health information regarding coronary artery disease prevention.

Worsening Anginal Symptoms
Anginal symptoms that require referral to a cardiologist.

Acute Coronary Syndrome
ACS is a group of conditions that share a common pathophysiology involving the rupture of a vulnerable plaque.<SEP>Acute Coronary Syndrome is the main medical condition discussed, requiring intensive monitoring and specific therapeutic interventions.<SEP>A medical condition that requires immediate referral to a cardiologist.

Cardiologist
A cardiologist is a specialist who evaluates patients, especially those with worsening symptoms, for advanced cardiac interventions.<SEP>A medical specialist to whom patients with certain symptoms or syndromes should be referred.

Haemodynamic Instability
A finding during physical examination that healthcare providers should look for.

Pulmonary Congestion
A finding during physical examination that healthcare providers should look for.<SEP>The accumulation of fluid in the lungs, which is a cause of dyspnea.

Ischaemic Mitral Regurgitation
A cardiac condition suggesting papillary muscle dysfunction or rupture.

Acute MI
Acute Myocardial Infarction, the diagnosis of which is characterized by changes in ECG waves.

Electrocardiogram
The medical tracing that serves as the hallmark diagnosis for Acute MI due to characteristic wave changes.

ST-T-Q Wave Changes
Changes (elevation, depression, inversion) detectable on the ECG across different phases of Acute MI.

Left Bundle Branch Block
A diagnostic sign on ECG, often indicating a large anterior wall infarction.<SEP>A form of heart block observed on an ECG that requires medical consideration.

Reperfusion Therapy
The primary treatment goal for patients with Acute MI, aiming to reduce irreversible myocardial damage.

Fibrinolytic Therapy
A type of therapy to be initiated early in the treatment of Acute MI.

Thrombolytic Therapy
A type of therapy for Acute MI, subject to specific eligibility criteria.

Cardiacogenic Shock
A serious clinical condition that a patient may present with after an acute MI.

Door-To-Needle Time
The critical timeframe of 20 to 30 minutes that should encompass initial evaluation for acute MI patients.

Papillary Muscle Rupture
A severe cause of mitral regurgitation.

Systolic Murmurs
New murmurs found during physical examination, suggesting underlying cardiac issues.

ST-Segment Elevation
A specific ECG finding that can be highly indicative of acute infarction.

Septum
The anatomical area whose rupture or infarction can lead to cardiac defects.

T-Wave Inversion
A specific ECG finding that indicates the normal return of the ST segment over time.

QS Wave
A specific ECG finding that may replace the R wave as a result of transmural necrosis.

12-Lead ECG Tracing
A comprehensive ECG recording used to determine the localization and extent of an infarction.

Papillary Muscle Dysfunction
A medical issue suggesting dysfunction or rupture that can cause mitral regurgitation.

Acute Coronary Syndromes
A set of acute events sharing a common pathophysiology involving the rupture of plaque and subsequent thrombotic cascades.

Atherosclerotic Plaque
A lipid-rich intracoronary deposit that, upon rupture, initiates the chain of events leading to acute coronary syndromes.

Myocardial Ischaemia
Myocardial ischaemia occurs when myocardial oxygen delivery cannot meet metabolic myocardial demands, resulting in subsequent angina.<SEP>Myocardial ischaemia results from a mismatch between coronary blood flow and myocardial oxygen demand.

Thrombus
A formed structure, usually occluding, that results from platelet aggregation and the inflammatory cascade following plaque rupture.

Myocardial Oxygen Delivery
The supply of oxygen to the myocardium, primarily determined by the oxygen-carrying capacity of blood and coronary flow.

Exercise Stress Testing (EST)
Exercise Stress Testing (EST) is a sensitive and informative examination useful for the detection and quantification of chronic ischaemic heart disease<SEP>A sensitive examination used for the detection and quantification of chronic ischaemic heart disease in at-risk patients.

Cardiac Enzymes
Serial measurements of cardiac enzymes are used to demonstrate cell injury and diagnose non-ST elevation MI.

Infarcation
Transmural myocardial injury occurring if the vessel lumen is completely occluded or inadequate collateralisation is present.

Unstable Angina
A clinical condition diagnosed if ischaemia occurs without demonstrable cell injury, noted by negative serial enzyme measurements.<SEP>Unstable angina is one type of acute coronary syndrome included under the definition of Non ST elevation acute coronary syndrome.

Supply-Demand Mismatch
This mismatch occurs when myocardial oxygen delivery cannot meet the metabolic myocardial demands, leading to ischaemia.

Coronary Flow Reserve
Coronary flow reserve is the capacity of coronary blood flow to increase three to fivefold in response to exercise.

Stenosis
Significant atherosclerotic plaquing in the epicardial coronary arteries, causing a drop in blood pressure across the affected lesion.

Inflammatory Cascade
A sequence of biochemical and biological events, including cytokine release and platelet activation, initiated by plaque rupture.

data.entity_name: Ministry Of Health And Quality Of Life/Mauritius Institute Of Health/World Health Organisation
data.content:
Ministry Of Health And Quality Of Life/Mauritius Institute Of Health/World Health Organisation
This organization is listed as the source for the clinical guidelines provided.<SEP>This organization published the guidelines regarding ACS diagnosis and treatment.<SEP>The organization published medical guidelines regarding cardiac interventions and associated contraindications.<SEP>The organization published guidelines for Primary and Secondary Prevention programs for Non-Communicable Diseases (NCD) and Cardiovascular Disease.<SEP>This organization is the source of specific articles related to health topics.

Acute Myocardial Infarction
AMI is a cardiac event that can be indicated by ST segment elevation and requires various interventions.

ST Segment Elevation
Elevation of the ST segment exceeding 1.0 mV in two continuous ECG leads is indicative of AMI.

Cardiogenic Shock
A severe condition requiring specific immediate treatment protocols.

Atrial Fibrillation
An irregular heart rhythm requiring specialized treatment, such as cardioversion.

Thrombolysis
A treatment process used for cardiac events, which carries risks of hemorrhage.

Primary Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI)
PCI is a procedure that can serve as an alternative to thrombolysis under specific conditions.

Atropine
A drug used in the treatment of bradyarrhythmia and heart block.

Pacing
A procedure recommended for bradyarrhythmia and heart block when atropine is insufficient.

Cardioversion
An electrical treatment used to manage Atrial Fibrillation and hemodynamic instability.

Intracerebral Hemorrhage
A serious bleeding event in the brain that is a risk associated with fibrinolytic agents.

Suspected Aortic Dissection
A condition that is an absolute contraindication for initiating specific treatments due to high risk.

Intracranial Neoplasm
A known tumor in the brain that is an absolute contraindication for certain treatments.

Haemorrhagic CVA
History of any hemorrhagic cerebrovascular accident is listed as an absolute contraindication.

Streptokinase
A type of thrombolytic agent requiring caution due to potential allergic reactions or prior exposure.

Anistreplase
A nonselected thrombolytic agent mentioned in the context of patient treatment.

Urokinase
A nonselected thrombolytic agent mentioned in the context of patient treatment.

Severe Hypertension
High blood pressure (BP > 180/110 mm.Hg.) that is a relative contraindication for treatment.

Bilateral Bundle Branch Block
A cardiac condition requiring investigation and management with pacing.

Dyspnea
The primary symptom of congestive heart failure, caused by pulmonary congestion and tachycardia.

Left Ventricular Function
The contractile performance of the left ventricle, which dictates the benefit of ACE inhibitors.

Gastric Pain
Gastric Pain is a symptom suspected in patients requiring potential treatment with an oral antacid.

Oral Antacid
An oral antacid is a treatment that can be administered if oesophageal or gastric pain is suspected.

Tachycardia
Tachycardia is a condition involving an elevated heart rate, suggesting a high-risk status requiring beta-blockers.<SEP>Tachycardia is a physiological sign that may be present during acute coronary syndromes.

Persistent Chest Pain
Persistent Chest Pain is a recurring symptom used as a high-risk feature that indicates the need for further therapy.

Ischaemic Ecg Changes
Ischaemic ECG changes are changes observed on the electrocardiogram, signifying underlying ischemia and indicating high risk.

Intravenous Beta-blockers
Intravenous beta-blockers are medications administered when multiple high-risk symptoms are present, such as severe hypertension or tachycardia.

data.entity_name: Ministry of Health And Quality Of Life/Mauritius Institute Of Health/World Health Organisation
data.content:
Ministry of Health And Quality Of Life/Mauritius Institute Of Health/World Health Organisation
This organization is the source of the medical guidelines and clinical protocols regarding ACS management.

Oesophageal Pain
Oesophageal Pain is a symptom suspected in patients requiring potential treatment with an oral antacid.

Oral Beta-blockade
Oral beta-blockade, exemplified by atenolol, is a prophylactic treatment recommended for all patients without contraindications.

Atenolol
Atenolol is a specific beta-blocker dosage (25–50 mg once daily) used for oral blockade in stable patients.

Antithrombotic Therapy
Antithrombotic therapy is a group of treatments indicated for high-risk ACS patients, preventing blood clots.

Anticoagulant Therapy
Anticoagulant therapy is a group of treatments indicated for high-risk ACS patients, preventing blood clots.

Unfractionated Heparin
Unfractionated heparin is a mainstay anticoagulant used in the treatment of ACS, often compared against newer agents.

Low Molecular Weight Heparins
Low molecular weight heparins are a newer class of anticoagulants that demonstrate reduced risk compared to unfractionated heparin.

Enoxaparin (Lovenox)
Enoxaparin, marketed as Lovenox, is a specific low molecular weight heparin shown to reduce combined endpoints

Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa Receptor
The receptor represents the final common pathway for platelet aggregation via fibrin binding and cross-linking.

Antibody Fragment (abciximab)
Antibody fragment abciximab is an antagonist developed to inhibit platelet aggregation via the GP IIb/IIIa receptor.

Peptide (Eptifibatide, Tirofiban)
Peptide antagonists, specifically eptifibatide and tirofiban, are used to inhibit platelet aggregation at the GP IIb/IIIa receptor.

Cardiac Catheterization
Cardiac catheterization is a procedure typically indicated if chest pain persists or recurs, alongside objective evidence of ischemia.

Revascularization
Revascularization is a procedure that should be considered if suitable coronary anatomy is present, potentially following cardiac catheterization.

Smoking
Smoking is listed as a cardiac risk factor that must be controlled during secondary prevention of ACS.<SEP>Smoking is a health hazard responsible for a significant portion of all avoidable deaths, particularly cardiovascular death.<SEP>Smoking is identified as a risk factor for CVD, necessitating complete cessation.

Diabetes
Diabetes is identified as a cardiac risk factor that requires control during secondary prevention of ACS.<SEP>Diabetes requires appropriate Oral Hypoglycemic Agent (OHA) use to achieve near normal Fasting Plasma Glucose (FPG).

Prothrombin Time
Prothrombin time is a target monitoring value during advanced ACS therapy, aiming for 1.5 to 2 times the control range.

N-3-omega Fatty Acids
N-3-omega fatty acids are a type of fat, along with monounsaturated fats and antioxidants, found in cardioprotective diets.

Mediterranean Diet
The Mediterranean diet is characterized by being low in saturated fat and high in unsaturated fat, and is associated with good life expectancy.<SEP>The Mediterranean Diet is associated with the best life expectancy, characterized by low saturated fat and high unsaturated fat, especially monounsaturated fat.

Japanese Diet
The Japanese diet is characterized by being low in saturated fat and high in complex carbohydrates, and is associated with good life expectancy.<SEP>The Japanese Diet is associated with the best life expectancy, characterized by low saturated fat and high complex carbohydrates.

Saturated Fatty Acids
Saturated fatty acids are a dietary component that should be kept low in diets associated with best life expectancy.

Unsaturated Fatty Acids
Unsaturated fatty acids are a beneficial dietary component, particularly monounsaturated types, associated with good life expectancy.

Complex Carbohydrates
Complex carbohydrates are a beneficial dietary component, key to the Japanese diet, and associated with good life expectancy.

Body Mass Index
Body mass index is a measure of obesity calculated using weight in kilograms divided by the square of height in meters, and is recommended by the WHO expert committee.

Waist Circumference
Waist circumference is a recommended measurement for clinical assessment and follow-up during weight reduction for determining the degree of obesity.

Waist Hip Ratio
The waist hip ratio is an index used in epidemiology to assess central obesity, defined as fat accumulation to the trunk and abdominal cavity.

Central Obesity
Central obesity is the accumulation of fat to the trunk and abdominal cavity and is associated with high prevalence of lipid abnormalities and hypertension.

Type 1 Diabetes
Type 1 diabetes is a condition associated with a marked increase of CHD, with risk becoming evident after the age of 30.

Type 2 Diabetes
Type 2 diabetes is a condition associated with more profound abnormalities in cardiovascular risk factors compared to Type 1 diabetes.

Alcohol
Alcohol is a substance whose consumption relationship with the risk of all cause mortality is described as J-shaped or U-shaped.

Monounsaturated Fat
Monounsaturated fat is a specific type of unsaturated fat considered beneficial, especially in the Mediterranean diet.

Dietician
A dietician is a professional source of assistance recommended for dietary interviews.

Trained Nurse
A specially trained nurse is a professional source of assistance recommended for dietary interviews.

BMI Classification
A classification scheme for assessing obesity based on specific weight/height ranges (e.g., Normal weight, Grade 3 overweight).

Impaired Glucose Tolerance
Impaired glucose tolerance is a health condition with recorded prevalence rates in adults aged 25 and above.<SEP>IGT is a condition associated with a cardiovascular risk pattern characteristic of type 2 diabetes.

Lipid Abnormalities
Lipid abnormalities are high prevalence risks associated with central obesity.

Passive Smoking
Passive smoking is identified as a risk factor that increases the risk of cardiovascular disease.

Myocardial Necrosis
Myocardial necrosis is a potential complication of rapid blood pressure reduction that reduces coronary autoregulation effectiveness.

Coronary Atherosclerosis
Coronary atherosclerosis is a potential complication of rapid blood pressure reduction that reduces coronary autoregulation effectiveness.

Cardiac Hypertrophy
Cardiac hypertrophy is a potential complication of rapid blood pressure reduction that reduces coronary autoregulation effectiveness.

Angioplasty
Angioplasty is a medical procedure mentioned in the context of coronary artery disease.

Bypass Grafting
Bypass grafting is a medical procedure aimed at treating coronary artery disease.

Risk Factor
A risk factor describes characteristics found in healthy individuals that are independently related to the subsequent occurrence of coronary heart disease.

Screening
Screening for coronary artery disease is a technique that must be easy, precise, cost-effective, and acceptable to the community.

Lifestyle Modification
Lifestyle modification can retard the development of coronary heart disease, both before and after a clinical event.

data.entity_name: Non St Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome
data.content:
Non St Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome
Non ST elevation acute coronary syndrome is defined by changes in a patient's angina status and includes presentations like new-onset angina and increasing severity of angina.

Non-St Elevation Myocardial Infarction
NSTEMI is a type of acute coronary syndrome defined by changes in a patient's angina status.

Physical Examination
Physical examination is useful for diagnosing non-ischaemic causes of pain, such as costochondritis and aortic dissection.

Complete Blood Count
CBC is a useful diagnostic test used to evaluate other causes of symptoms and concurrent medical conditions.

Electrolytes
Electrolytes are useful data measured during testing to evaluate concurrent medical conditions.

Renal Function Tests
Renal function tests are useful for diagnosing other causes of symptoms and evaluating for renal insufficiency.

Chest X-Ray
A chest x-ray is a useful test for diagnosing other causes of symptoms and evaluating for concurrent medical conditions.

Clotting Times
Clotting times should be obtained to exclude bleeding diatheses before antithrombotic therapy.

ECG Analysis
ECG analysis is a cornerstone diagnostic testing method essential for risk stratification in acute coronary syndromes.

Serum Cardiac Markers
Markers like CK-MB and troponin I or T are cornerstone diagnostic tests used to determine myocardial injury.

Non-Ischaemic Causes Of Pain
This concept includes conditions such as costochondritis, pneumonia, pericarditis, aortic dissection, and upper gastrointestinal disease, which can mimic ACS pain.

Nitroglycerin
Nitroglycerin (GTN, ISDN) is a drug used for pain relief and cardiac treatment, administered via intravenous or sublingual routes.

Beta Blockers
Beta blockers are medications used for cardiac treatment and can be used unless contraindicated.

Low Molecular Weight Heparin
Enoxaparin is a specific anticoagulant prescribed in the management of ACS, typically for 48 hours.

Ticlopidine
Ticlopidine is an alternative medication for ACS if aspirin is contraindicated.

Glycoprotein 11b- 111a Receptor Inhibitors
Reopro or Integrelin are medications that act as inhibitors for the glycoprotein 11b- 111a receptor in ACS treatment.

Bradycardia
Bradycardia is a physiological sign that may be present during acute coronary syndromes.

Peripheral Vascular Disease
Peripheral vascular disease includes signs like carotid bruits or diminished extremity pulses, which increase suspicion of coronary disease.

Atherosclerotic Coronary Disease
This is a disease whose suspicion should be increased by signs of peripheral vascular disease.

Costochondritis
Costochondritis is a non-ischaemic cause of pain that physical examination is useful for diagnosing.

Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
Obstructive pulmonary disease is a non-ischaemic cause of pain that physical examination can help diagnose.

Pericarditis
Pericarditis is a non-ischaemic cause of pain that physical examination can help diagnose.

Upper Gastrointestinal Disease
Upper gastrointestinal disease is a non-ischaemic cause of pain that physical examination can help diagnose.

Aortic Dissection
Aortic dissection is a serious non-ischaemic cause of pain that physical examination can help diagnose.

Biphasic Or Inverted T Waves
These are ECG abnormalities showing ischemic changes and are predictive of increased risk.

Smoking Cessation
Smoking Cessation is a topic addressed within the cited literature regarding health interventions.

Chrostopher P Housen
Chrostopher P Housen is an author associated with research on cardiovascular disease control in developing countries.

Bulletin Of World Health Federation
This organization publishes a bulletin regarding health research and development.

data.entity_name: Physical Activity For Healthy Adults As A Primary Prevention Intervention In The Clinical Setting
data.content:
Physical Activity For Healthy Adults As A Primary Prevention Intervention In The Clinical Setting
This content addresses physical activity as a primary prevention method for healthy adults.

Sally S Haris
Sally S Haris is an author associated with the content concerning physical activity and primary prevention.

Kikua Arakawa
Kikua Arakawa is an author associated with the content regarding physical exercise and hypertension management.

data.entity_name: Physical Exercise In The Management Of Hypertension
data.content:
Physical Exercise In The Management Of Hypertension
This content discusses the role of physical exercise specifically in managing hypertension.

Behavioural Change
Behavioural change is the central focus, suggesting that individuals are not equally ready to modify their behavior.

Procjaska
Procjaska is a theorist who, along with Diclemente, proposed the Stages of Change Model.

Diclemente
Diclemente is a theorist who, along with Procjaska, proposed the Stages of Change Model.

Stages Of Change Model
The Stages of Change Model is a theory proposing that behavioral modification requires assessing an individual's readiness to change.

Pre-Contemplation
Pre-contemplation is the initial stage where individuals do not intend to change their high-risk behavior in the foreseeable future.

Contemplation
Contemplation is the stage where people seriously intend to change their behavior but often stay in this stage for extended periods due to ambivalence.

Preparation Action
Preparation action is one of the five proposed stages of behavioral change.

Maintenance
Maintenance is one of the five proposed stages of behavioral change, indicating the sustained lifestyle change.

Chronic Ischaemic Heart Disease
This is the primary condition requiring multifactorial intervention, regular follow-up, and medical management.<SEP>Chronic Ischaemic Heart Disease is the underlying condition treated by the discussed medical therapies, aiming to prevent MI and death.

Chest Pain
Chest Pain is a symptom that requires differential diagnosis evaluation in patients suspected of having IHD.

Myocardial Infarction (MI)
Myocardial Infarction (MI) is a major event that treatment strategies aim to prevent in patients with IHD.

Beta-Blockers
Beta-blockers are a class of drugs used to decrease heart rate, contractility, and arterial pressure for treating angina and hypertension.

Severe Aortic Stenosis
Severe aortic stenosis is listed as a primary cardiac disorder and an absolute contraindication for EST.

Hypertrophic Obstructive Cardiomyopathy (HOCM)
HOCM is listed as a primary cardiac disorder and an absolute contraindication for EST.

Coronary Revascularization
Coronary revascularization is one of two treatment methods for IHD, alongside medical therapy.

data.entity_name: Ministry Of Health And Quality Of Life / Mauritius Institute Of Health / World Health Organisation
data.content:
Ministry Of Health And Quality Of Life / Mauritius Institute Of Health / World Health Organisation
Organizations responsible for the content published in the medical guidelines.

Non-Communicable Disease
NCD is the focus area of the guidelines, requiring primary and secondary prevention.

Physical Activity
Physical Activity intervention is recommended for CVD patients, aiming for minimum 30-60 minutes of moderate intensity exercise per week.

Lipid Management
Lipid Management guidelines recommend specific dietary restrictions and drug therapies to achieve target cholesterol levels (e.g., LDL < 100 mg/dl).

Oestrogen Replacement Therapy
Oestrogen Replacement Therapy is specifically considered for post-menopausal women who have multiple risk factors for CHD.

Antiplatelets/Anticoagulants
These agents include starting ASA 75 to 300 mg or managing warfarin to maintain an international normalized ratio of 2 to 3.5.

Primary Prevention
Primary Prevention strategies are outlined in the guidelines for reducing NCD and CVD risk factors.

Secondary Prevention
Secondary Prevention strategies are outlined in the guidelines for managing existing NCD and CVD risks.

Triglyceride Level
Triglyceride levels are measured for lipid management, with thresholds ranging from < 200 mg/dl to > 400 mg/dl.

Weight Management
Weight Management is required for CVD patients, emphasizing the need for weight loss due to associated increased TG and glucose levels.

Blood Pressure Control
Blood Pressure Control involves lifestyle modification and individualized antihypertensive drug use to maintain a blood pressure below 140/90 mmHg.

LDL Cholesterol
Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol has specific target ranges, including < 100 mg/dl and 100-130 mg/dl, crucial for lipid management.

Blood Pressure Readings
Specific blood pressure targets are set for hypertension control, generally recommending a goal equal or less than 140/90 mmHg.

Statin Drug Therapy
Statin treatment is a suggested drug therapy for lipid management when LDL goals are not achieved.

Fibrate Drug Therapy
Fibrate therapy is listed as a suggested drug therapy option for managing high triglycerides.

Niacin Drug Therapy
Niacin therapy is a suggested drug therapy option for lipid management, sometimes combined with statin treatment.

International Normalized Ratio
The International Normalized Ratio (INR) is a measurement used for managing warfarin, requiring a target range of 2 to 3.5 for post-MI patients.

Glucose Control
Glucose control is one of the components of medical treatment for chronic ischaemic heart disease.

Lipid Profile
Lipid profile management is recommended as part of medical treatment for chronic ischaemic heart disease.

Exercise
Exercise is a recommended component of a regular program for patients with chronic ischaemic heart disease.

Endurance
Endurance is a measure that should be assessed for patients with chronic ischaemic heart disease.

Objective Measures Of Ischaemia
These measures are used to assess the status of patients with chronic ischaemic heart disease.

Follow-Up Evaluation
Follow-up evaluation is recommended for successfully treated patients every 4 to 12 months to monitor status.

Anginal Symptoms
Anginal symptoms are symptoms that must be monitored regularly during follow-up evaluation.

Unstable Angina (UA)
Unstable Angina is a type of non-ST elevation acute coronary syndrome defined by changes in a patient’s angina status.

ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (MI)
ST elevation myocardial infarction is a type of non-ST elevation acute coronary syndrome identified by serial cardiac enzyme measurements showing cell injury.

Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF)
LVEF is a measurement, specifically less than 35%, that suggests a need for coronary angiography consideration.

Coronary Angiography
Coronary angiography is a diagnostic procedure considered for patients meeting specific risk criteria.

Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
CABG is a surgical procedure considered for patients with multi-vessel coronary artery disease (CAD) who have failed medical management.

Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI)
PCI is an interventional procedure considered for patients with multi-vessel CAD who have suitable anatomy.

Aspirin And Anti-Anginals
Aspirin and anti-anginals are required components of the medical treatment plan for chronic ischaemic heart disease.

Beta-Blocker
Beta-blockers are recommended components of the medical treatment plan for chronic ischaemic heart disease.

Cholesterol Lowering
Cholesterol lowering is a vital part of managing chronic ischaemic heart disease.

Diabetic Assessment
Assessment of diabetes is necessary for the management of chronic ischaemic heart disease.

data.entity_name: Canadian Cardiovascular Society Angina Class III Or IV
data.content:
Canadian Cardiovascular Society Angina Class III Or IV
This class rating is used to categorize patients with worsening anginal symptoms requiring referral.

Primary Care Physicians
Primary care physicians and cardiologists may co-manage patients by alternating follow-up evaluations.

Coronary Blood Flow
Myocardial ischaemia results when the oxygen delivery, influenced by coronary flow, cannot meet the metabolic demands.

Aspirin, Heparin
Use of GP IIb/IIIa antagonists should be combined with aspirin and heparin for suspected non-ST elevation ACS.

Hypertension, Tachycardia
Intravenous beta-blockers are indicated when significant hypertension, tachycardia, or other symptoms are present.

Monounsaturated Fats
N-3-omega fatty acids are listed as components of cardioprotective diets.

Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa Receptor Antagonists
Use of GP IIb/IIIa antagonists should be combined with aspirin and heparin for suspected non-ST elevation ACS.

Smoking, Hyperlipidemia
The secondary prevention of ACS includes controlling cardiac risk factors such as smoking and hyperlipidemia.

Body Weight
Body mass index calculation requires body weight in kilograms and height in meters.

EST
EST is used to evaluate patients with symptoms suggestive of chest pain.

Medical Therapy
Medical therapy combined with aggressive cardiovascular risk factor modification is the cornerstone of IHD treatment.

Heart Failure
Beta-blockers have been shown to reduce total mortality in patients with compensated heart failure.
